title: Quiz: Real-Time Feedback and Adaptive Control
description: Test your understanding of feedback systems, active reset, mid-circuit measurement, and latency requirements for quantum control

questions.question: Why must quantum feedback latency be much shorter than the qubit coherence time T2?

options.text: To save electricity by completing operations faster
Because the qubit loses quantum information during the feedback delay, limiting useful computation
To match the speed of classical computers
Because FPGA clock speeds require fast operations

questions.explanation: The qubit continuously decoheres during the feedback delay. If feedback takes 1 microsecond and T2 is 100 microseconds, you lose 1% of coherence just waiting for feedback. The goal is to minimize this overhead so most of the coherence time is available for useful quantum operations.

questions.question: In active qubit reset, what happens when the measurement indicates the qubit is in state |1>?

options.text: The system waits for thermal relaxation to |0>
A conditional X (pi) pulse is applied to flip the qubit to |0>
The qubit is discarded and a new one is used
A Z gate is applied to change the phase

questions.explanation: Active reset applies a conditional X (pi) pulse when the qubit is measured in |1>. This flips the qubit to |0> much faster than waiting for thermal relaxation (which would take 5-10 times T1, hundreds of microseconds). Active reset can achieve >99.9% ground state preparation in about 1 microsecond.

questions.question: What is the typical feedback latency for a state-of-the-art superconducting qubit system?

options.text: 1-10 nanoseconds
200-600 nanoseconds
10-50 microseconds
1-10 milliseconds

questions.explanation: State-of-the-art systems achieve feedback latencies of 200-600 nanoseconds. This includes readout resonator ring-up (~300 ns), signal integration (~100-300 ns), and FPGA processing (~50-100 ns). This is fast enough for real-time feedback within typical T2 times of 50-200 microseconds.

questions.question: In the SNR vs integration time trade-off, how does the signal-to-noise ratio scale with the number of integrated samples?

options.text: Linearly: SNR increases proportionally with samples
As square root: SNR scales as sqrt(N) for N samples
Quadratically: SNR scales as N^2
Logarithmically: SNR scales as log(N)

questions.explanation: For white noise, SNR improves as the square root of the number of samples: SNR(N) = SNR(1) * sqrt(N). This means quadrupling integration time only doubles the SNR. This square root scaling is why there's a trade-off between integration time (latency) and measurement fidelity.

questions.question: Mid-circuit measurements can only be performed on ancilla qubits, never on data qubits.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False. Mid-circuit measurements can be performed on any qubit, including data qubits. However, measuring a data qubit projects it into a classical state, which may not be desirable. Ancilla qubits are often used to extract information without directly measuring the data qubits (as in syndrome measurements for error correction).

questions.question: For syndrome-based quantum error correction with real-time feedback, what is the primary challenge as the code size increases?

options.text: Larger codes require more expensive cryogenic equipment
Decoder latency increases and must remain within the coherence time budget
The qubits become physically larger
Larger codes cannot be implemented on FPGAs

questions.explanation: As quantum error correction codes grow, the decoder must process more syndromes and solve increasingly complex optimization problems. For example, a surface code decoder might need microseconds or more, which consumes a significant fraction of the coherence time budget. This decoder latency scaling is a major challenge for fault-tolerant quantum computing.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid techniques to reduce feedback latency? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Reduce integration time (trading off measurement fidelity)
Use lookup tables instead of algorithmic decoding
Increase qubit T2 coherence time
Pipeline FPGA processing stages

questions.explanation: Options A, B, and D directly reduce feedback latency. Reducing integration time trades SNR for speed. Lookup tables provide O(1) decoding for small codes. Pipelining allows overlapping operations. Option C (increasing T2) doesn't reduce latency but increases the latency budget - it helps but doesn't address the latency itself.

questions.question: What is AC Stark shift in the context of mid-circuit measurement, and why is it a concern?

options.text: A shift in the laser wavelength that reduces measurement fidelity
A frequency shift of qubits caused by off-resonant drive signals, leading to unwanted phase accumulation
A shift in the FPGA clock frequency under high load
A thermal shift in component values as the system warms up

questions.explanation: AC Stark shift is a frequency shift caused by off-resonant electromagnetic drives. During mid-circuit measurement, strong readout pulses can cause AC Stark shifts on neighboring qubits, leading to unwanted phase accumulation and errors. This is why careful frequency planning and crosstalk mitigation are essential.
